texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane reactors have been selected for the hydrogen generation from hydrocarbon reforming reaction being analyzed for the following reasons: (a) Membrane separation is a promising and developing technology compared with the other two methods (pressure-swing-adsorption to separate H2 and pressure-swingadsorption to separate CO2) and (b) the operation conditions for the H2 separation process of the analyzed reforming reactor are at the limit of the typical operation conditions of the H2 membrane separators (temperature between 300 and 600  Candpressure difference across the membrane between 1 and 40 bar) [1]. Pd and its alloy membranes have been developed to separate H2 from gas mixtures andinmembranereactors[2].Inaddition,theyhavebeenwidelyused by hydrogenation and dehydrogenation industries [3 e 6].
texts.text: The membrane reactors have been selected for the hydrogen generation from hydrocarbon reforming reaction being analyzed for the following reasons: (a) Membrane separation is a promising and developing technology compared with the other two methods (pressure-swing-adsorption to separate H2 and pressure-swingadsorption to separate CO2) and (b) the operation conditions for the H2 separation process of the analyzed reforming reactor are at the limit of the typical operation conditions of the H2 membrane separators (temperature between 300 and 600  Candpressure difference across the membrane between 1 and 40 bar) [1]. Pd and its alloy membranes have been developed to separate H2 from gas mixtures andinmembranereactors[2].Inaddition,theyhavebeenwidelyused by hydrogenation and dehydrogenation industries [3 e 6].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hydrogen and carbon generation from the catalytic decomposition of light hydrocarbons has attracted great attention in recent year as a potentially clean energy source of hydrogen and industrially application of carbon [7]. Hydrogen is used as a clean energy source and carbon can be used in functional materials, such as fi bers, graphite, carbon black, composite, and electrodes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hydrogen separation from the re fi nery off-gas of the ethylene decomposition was carried out with Pd-1wt.%Au alloy membranes deposited on a porous nickel support using the sputtering and polishing method. In order to verify the effect of ethylene on the membrane, hydrogen permeation tests were carried out with 5% ethylene mixtures at temperatures ranging from 573 to 673 K and pressure differences ranging from 500 to 1500 kPa. Hydrogen permeation tests, which were carried out with the membranes that had an effective area of 16.6 cm 2 , revealed that the ethylene mixture did not affect hydrogen permeation behavior when the feed streams were 3000 ml min � 1 and the pressure difference was 500 kPa at 673 K. As the pressure difference and temperature increased and the feed fl ow rate decreased, hydrogen permeation fl ux became less than ideal, indicating that depletion was a major factor in the hydrogen permeation behavior. From the aspect of process design, the membrane with an effective area of 16.6 cm 2 could enrich 5% ethylene of 2000 ml min � 1 up to 76, 80, and 82% at 573 K, 623 K, and 673 K, respectively with a hydrogen recovery ratio of > 96% at a pressure difference of 1500 kPa.
texts.text: Hydrogen separation from the re fi nery off-gas of the ethylene decomposition was carried out with Pd-1wt.%Au alloy membranes deposited on a porous nickel support using the sputtering and polishing method. In order to verify the effect of ethylene on the membrane, hydrogen permeation tests were carried out with 5% ethylene mixtures at temperatures ranging from 573 to 673 K and pressure differences ranging from 500 to 1500 kPa. Hydrogen permeation tests, which were carried out with the membranes that had an effective area of 16.6 cm 2 , revealed that the ethylene mixture did not affect hydrogen permeation behavior when the feed streams were 3000 ml min � 1 and the pressure difference was 500 kPa at 673 K. As the pressure difference and temperature increased and the feed fl ow rate decreased, hydrogen permeation fl ux became less than ideal, indicating that depletion was a major factor in the hydrogen permeation behavior. From the aspect of process design, the membrane with an effective area of 16.6 cm 2 could enrich 5% ethylene of 2000 ml min � 1 up to 76, 80, and 82% at 573 K, 623 K, and 673 K, respectively with a hydrogen recovery ratio of > 96% at a pressure difference of 1500 kPa.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: [7,8]. The light hydrocarbons used for catalytic decomposition include methane [9], ethane [10], propane [11], ethylene [12 e 15], and acetylene [16]. Off-gas streams from re fi neries contain hydrogen and hydrocarbons, thus, this could be great source of hydrogen and carbon if methods to effectively separate hydrogen from these off-gas streams are developed. Pd-based membranes hold great promise for use in separating hydrogen from off-gas streams. Some studies regarding the effect of hydrocarbons on the hydrogen permeation behavior and membrane characterization have been reported. Ziemecki et al. observed that hydrocarbons reacted with palladium to form PdC [17]. Sheintuch et al. showed that isobutane and propane inhibit hydrogen dissociation and recombination, resulting in a decrease of hydrogen permeation fl ux [11]. Jung et al. observed that methane and propane did not affect hydrogen permeance but propylene strongly decreased hydrogen permeance [18]. Li et al. reported that methane decomposition occurred and fi lamentous carbon was formed on the membrane surface during hydrogen permeation tests in methane mixtures [19].
texts.text: [7,8]. The light hydrocarbons used for catalytic decomposition include methane [9], ethane [10], propane [11], ethylene [12 e 15], and acetylene [16]. Off-gas streams from re fi neries contain hydrogen and hydrocarbons, thus, this could be great source of hydrogen and carbon if methods to effectively separate hydrogen from these off-gas streams are developed. Pd-based membranes hold great promise for use in separating hydrogen from off-gas streams. Some studies regarding the effect of hydrocarbons on the hydrogen permeation behavior and membrane characterization have been reported. Ziemecki et al. observed that hydrocarbons reacted with palladium to form PdC [17]. Sheintuch et al. showed that isobutane and propane inhibit hydrogen dissociation and recombination, resulting in a decrease of hydrogen permeation fl ux [11]. Jung et al. observed that methane and propane did not affect hydrogen permeance but propylene strongly decreased hydrogen permeance [18]. Li et al. reported that methane decomposition occurred and fi lamentous carbon was formed on the membrane surface during hydrogen permeation tests in methane mixtures [19].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, the use of Pd-based membranes for hydrogen separation in an ethylene mixture was investigated. The goal was to study the feasibility of separating hydrogen for ethylene recycle and hydrogen puri fi cation. The gas composition of perm and retentate sides were analyzed to con fi rm the purity of hydrogen and the enrichment of ethylene, respectively. The membrane was analyzed by SEM/EDX to con fi rm the formation of carbon.
texts.text: In this study, the use of Pd-based membranes for hydrogen separation in an ethylene mixture was investigated. The goal was to study the feasibility of separating hydrogen for ethylene recycle and hydrogen puri fi cation. The gas composition of perm and retentate sides were analyzed to con fi rm the purity of hydrogen and the enrichment of ethylene, respectively. The membrane was analyzed by SEM/EDX to con fi rm the formation of carbon.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Au/Pd membranes were prepared by the sputtering and polishing method described in previous study [20]. Nickel powder with an average particle size of 3.0 m mwaspurchased from Inco Co. and used as the raw material for PNS. This powder has a spherical shape with a nickel purity of 99.9%. The nickel powder was compressed without a binder in a cylindrical metal mold that had a diameter of 50 mm using a homemade press under high pressure (333 MPa) and then the compressed support was treated at 1173 K under H2 for 2 h to improve its mechanical strength.
texts.text: Au/Pd membranes were prepared by the sputtering and polishing method described in previous study [20]. Nickel powder with an average particle size of 3.0 m mwaspurchased from Inco Co. and used as the raw material for PNS. This powder has a spherical shape with a nickel purity of 99.9%. The nickel powder was compressed without a binder in a cylindrical metal mold that had a diameter of 50 mm using a homemade press under high pressure (333 MPa) and then the compressed support was treated at 1173 K under H2 for 2 h to improve its mechanical strength.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PNS was modi fi ed with sub-micron sized nickel powder from Aldrich. A sub-micron sized nickel slurry was prepared by adding 5 g of sub-micron sized nickel powder into 100 ml acetone then dispersing the solution through ultrasonication. In order to assure that the sub-micron sized nickel particles were fi lled into the entrance pores of the PNS, a vacuum was applied to the other side of the PNS. Excess sub-micron sized nickel particles on the PNS were carefully removed. After drying at ambient temperature, the PNS was calcined at 700  C for 2 h in hydrogen.
texts.text: The PNS was modi fi ed with sub-micron sized nickel powder from Aldrich. A sub-micron sized nickel slurry was prepared by adding 5 g of sub-micron sized nickel powder into 100 ml acetone then dispersing the solution through ultrasonication. In order to assure that the sub-micron sized nickel particles were fi lled into the entrance pores of the PNS, a vacuum was applied to the other side of the PNS. Excess sub-micron sized nickel particles on the PNS were carefully removed. After drying at ambient temperature, the PNS was calcined at 700  C for 2 h in hydrogen.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 4 micron thick Pd and 50 nm thick Au layers were deposited on the modi fi ed porous nickel supports (PNS) using the magnetron sputtering method. To increase the adhesion and activation of the support, the surface was treated with H2/Ar plasma. This surface modifi cation step was carried out in a 10% H2/Ar mixture at a working pressure of 50 mTorr and radio frequency of 100 W for 5 min. A Pd target that was 1/8 inch in thickness and 3 inch in diameter (purchased form Kurt J. Lesker Co.) was used as the sputtering source. The supports were kept approximately 70 mm away from the sputtering gun during deposition. Before deposition, the sputtering chamber was evacuated to 1.0 -10 � 8 Torr and then fi lled with ultra high purity argon. All membranes were deposited at

To increase the adhesion and activation of the support, the surface was treated with H2/Ar plasma. texts.orig: Fig. 2. Hydrogen permeation fl ux through the membrane as a function of pressure difference with a pressure exponent of 0.6: Feed was pure hydrogen.
texts.text: Fig. 2. Hydrogen permeation fl ux through the membrane as a function of pressure difference with a pressure exponent of 0.6: Feed was pure hydrogen.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1 mTorr in ultra high purity argon. During sputtering, the temperature of the substrate was controlled at 450  C. After Pd and Au deposition, heat-treatment was performed at 450  C for 5 h. Subsequently, the sputtering chamber was gradually cooled to room temperature. The Pd and Au sputtered and heat-treated membranes were polished using an auto-polisher (GNP Tech, Poly-300). During the polishing process, alumina slurry was continuously added. Alumina slurry was made by mixing 100 g of alumina powder (average size of 0.9 m m)in 1000 ml deionized water. A polishing pad was rotate at a rotating-speed of 60 rpm with a loading pressure of 15 g cm � 2 . After the polishing process was complete (60 s), the membrane was carefully cleaned with de-ionized water several times and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60  C.
texts.text: 1 mTorr in ultra high purity argon. During sputtering, the temperature of the substrate was controlled at 450  C. After Pd and Au deposition, heat-treatment was performed at 450  C for 5 h. Subsequently, the sputtering chamber was gradually cooled to room temperature. The Pd and Au sputtered and heat-treated membranes were polished using an auto-polisher (GNP Tech, Poly-300). During the polishing process, alumina slurry was continuously added. Alumina slurry was made by mixing 100 g of alumina powder (average size of 0.9 m m)in 1000 ml deionized water. A polishing pad was rotate at a rotating-speed of 60 rpm with a loading pressure of 15 g cm � 2 . After the polishing process was complete (60 s), the membrane was carefully cleaned with de-ionized water several times and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60  C.

texts.orig: the membrane from undergoing a phase transition, which can cause membrane fracture [21]. The fl ow-rate of the perm-side was measured using a soap-bubble fl ow meter. The gas composition of the perm and retantate side was analyzed using two different gas chromatograph systems (Agilent 6890N). One of the systems was equipped with a Carboxen 1010 column and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and the other consisted of an HP PLOT Alumina column with a Flame Ionization Detector (FID). The temperature was controlled using a K -type thermocouple at the surface of the membrane. A schematic of the testing rig is shown in Fig. 1.
texts.text: the membrane from undergoing a phase transition, which can cause membrane fracture [21]. The fl ow-rate of the perm-side was measured using a soap-bubble fl ow meter. One of the systems was equipped with a Carboxen 1010 column and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and the other consisted of an HP PLOT Alumina column with a Flame Ionization Detector (FID). The temperature was controlled using a K -type thermocouple at the surface of the membrane. A schematic of the testing rig is shown in Fig. 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: metal layer is the rate-limiting step, then the hydrogen fl ux will depend on the pressure exponent of 0.5, as in Sieverts ' law. The pressure exponent calculated from the hydrogen fl ux is depicted in Fig. 2. As shown in this fi gure, a pressure exponent of 0.6 produced good fi ts. Membranes thinner than 4 e 5 m mappeared were fi t best with a hydrogen pressure exponent larger than 0.5 [22]. Hydrogen permeation due to either the surface reaction or to the combination of bulk diffusion and surface reaction will make the pressure exponent deviate from 0.5 [23]. In our previous study, we showed that the pressure exponent deviated from 0.5 because of support resistance [24], which indicates that the hydrogen permeation of the present membrane depends not only on atomic diffusion through the palladium fi lm but also on the surface process and/or support resistance.
texts.text: metal layer is the rate-limiting step, then the hydrogen fl ux will depend on the pressure exponent of 0.5, as in Sieverts ' law. The pressure exponent calculated from the hydrogen fl ux is depicted in Fig. 2. As shown in this fi gure, a pressure exponent of 0.6 produced good fi ts. Membranes thinner than 4 e 5 m mappeared were fi t best with a hydrogen pressure exponent larger than 0.5 [22]. Hydrogen permeation due to either the surface reaction or to the combination of bulk diffusion and surface reaction will make the pressure exponent deviate from 0.5 [23]. In our previous study, we showed that the pressure exponent deviated from 0.5 because of support resistance [24], which indicates that the hydrogen permeation of the present membrane depends not only on atomic diffusion through the palladium fi lm but also on the surface process and/or support resistance.

texts.orig: The hydrogen permeation values at 1500 kPa were 1.21, 1.47 and 1.69 mol m � 2 s � 1 at 573, 623 and 673 K respectively. As shown in Table 1, these values were higher than the results of a previous study on Pd membranes fabricated using a similar method [20]. These results are in good agreement with another study. Alloying with Au results in an initial increase in the permeability with a maximum permeability at 10wt.% Au [25].
texts.text: The hydrogen permeation values at 1500 kPa were 1.21, 1.47 and 1.69 mol m � 2 s � 1 at 573, 623 and 673 K respectively. As shown in Table 1, these values were higher than the results of a previous study on Pd membranes fabricated using a similar method [20]. These results are in good agreement with another study. Alloying with Au results in an initial increase in the permeability with a maximum permeability at 10wt.% Au [25].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The hydrogen separation behavior in the ethylene mixture was carried out at pressure differences ranging from 500 to 1500 kPa. In order to investigate the hydrogen extraction capacity of the membrane, the feed fl ow-rate was varied from 1000 to 3000 ml min � 1 . As expected, hydrogen permeation fl ux increased with an increase in the pressure difference and temperature as shown in Fig. 3. The magnitude of the increase in the permeation rate at higher pressure differences increased with increasing feed fl ow rate. When comparing the hydrogen permeation fl ux values of the gas mixtures with pure hydrogen, 5% ethylene did not have a signi fi cant impact at a feed fl ow rate, temperature, and pressure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: difference of 3000 ml min � 1 , 673 K, 500 kPa respectively. Yoshida et al. reported that impurities such NH3, CH4, CO, CO2, and N2 at low concentration ( < 1%) did not have an effect on the hydrogen permeability of a commercial Pd-25wt.%(Ag-Au-Ru) alloy membrane [26]. As the feed rate decreased and pressure difference increased, hydrogen permeation fl ux became less than the values determined with pure hydrogen. The reduction in the hydrogen permeation fl ux was due to depletion. Peters et al. revealed that depletion effects increased with increasing pressure differences using nitrogen and WGS mixtures [27]. When the feed fl ow was 1000 ml min � 1 , the increase in hydrogen permeation fl ux with temperature and pressure difference was very small.
texts.text: difference of 3000 ml min � 1 , 673 K, 500 kPa respectively. Yoshida et al. reported that impurities such NH3, CH4, CO, CO2, and N2 at low concentration ( < 1%) did not have an effect on the hydrogen permeability of a commercial Pd-25wt.%(Ag-Au-Ru) alloy membrane [26]. As the feed rate decreased and pressure difference increased, hydrogen permeation fl ux became less than the values determined with pure hydrogen. The reduction in the hydrogen permeation fl ux was due to depletion. Peters et al. revealed that depletion effects increased with increasing pressure differences using nitrogen and WGS mixtures [27]. When the feed fl ow was 1000 ml min � 1 , the increase in hydrogen permeation fl ux with temperature and pressure difference was very small.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This can be explained through the hydrogen recovery ratio shown in Fig. 4. The hydrogen recovery ratio increased with increasing temperature and pressure difference. When the feed fl ow was 1000 ml min � 1 , the hydrogen recovery ratio was > 90% in the temperature range of 583 e 673 K and the pressure difference range of 500 e 1000 kPa. In addition, the hydrogen permeation fl ux could not be increased when the pressure difference was > 1000 kPa at 583 and 623 K and > 700 kPa at 673 K. This indicates that most of hydrogen was extracted from the ethylene mixtures.
texts.text: This can be explained through the hydrogen recovery ratio shown in Fig. 4. The hydrogen recovery ratio increased with increasing temperature and pressure difference. When the feed fl ow was 1000 ml min � 1 , the hydrogen recovery ratio was > 90% in the temperature range of 583 e 673 K and the pressure difference range of 500 e 1000 kPa. In addition, the hydrogen permeation fl ux could not be increased when the pressure difference was > 1000 kPa at 583 and 623 K and > 700 kPa at 673 K. This indicates that most of hydrogen was extracted from the ethylene mixtures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to con fi rm the effect of the feed fl ow rate on the hydrogen permeation fl ux, the hydrogen permeation fl ux was plotted as a function of the feed fl ow rate. The effect of the feed fl ow rate on hydrogen permeation fl ux was obvious, but leveled off even at high rates as shown in Fig. 5. The obtained hydrogen permeation fl ux increased with an increase in the feed fl ow rate and this increase gradually leveled off. As expected, the hydrogen permeation fl ux continued to increase with a further increase in the feed fl ow rate. This was due to the decreasing effect of hydrogen depletion on the membrane surface resulting in a higher hydrogen partial pressure. While the hydrogen permeation fl ux increased with increasing feed fl ow rate, the hydrogen recovery ratio decreased as shown in Fig. 4. Based on the results of the hydrogen permeation tests, we can conclude that the effect of ethylene on the hydrogen permeation behavior depends only on the hydrogen partial pressure on the surface of the membranes. The hydrogen partial pressure on the surface is affected by the feed fl ow rate and operation conditions such as the temperature and pressure difference.
texts.text: In order to con fi rm the effect of the feed fl ow rate on the hydrogen permeation fl ux, the hydrogen permeation fl ux was plotted as a function of the feed fl ow rate. The effect of the feed fl ow rate on hydrogen permeation fl ux was obvious, but leveled off even at high rates as shown in Fig. 5. The obtained hydrogen permeation fl ux increased with an increase in the feed fl ow rate and this increase gradually leveled off. As expected, the hydrogen permeation fl ux continued to increase with a further increase in the feed fl ow rate. This was due to the decreasing effect of hydrogen depletion on the membrane surface resulting in a higher hydrogen partial pressure. While the hydrogen permeation fl ux increased with increasing feed fl ow rate, the hydrogen recovery ratio decreased as shown in Fig. 4. Based on the results of the hydrogen permeation tests, we can conclude that the effect of ethylene on the hydrogen permeation behavior depends only on the hydrogen partial pressure on the surface of the membranes. The hydrogen partial pressure on the surface is affected by the feed fl ow rate and operation conditions such as the temperature and pressure difference.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to examine ethylene enrichment using our membrane, the gas composition of the retentate streams were analyzed. As expected, the ethylene concentrations increased with an increase in the pressure difference as shown in Fig. 6. As the temperature increased, the required pressure difference for enrichment of ethylene decreased. This result corresponds to the hydrogen recovery shown in Fig. 4. When the feed fl ow rate was 1000mlmin � 1 , ethylene could be enriched up to w 80%at a pressure difference of 1000 kPa and 573 K. The ability of the membrane to recover hydrogenwhile allowing for ethylene enrichment must also beconsidered. In regards to the recovered hydrogen fl ow rate, a feed rate of 1000 ml min � 1 was too low. When the feed fl ow rate and pressure difference were 2000 ml min � 1 and 1500 kPa, respectively, the ethylene concentration in the retentate reached up to 76, 80, and 82% at 573 K, 623 K, and 673 K respectively. Under these conditions, the hydrogen recovery ratio was > 96% as shown in Fig. 4.
texts.text: In order to examine ethylene enrichment using our membrane, the gas composition of the retentate streams were analyzed. As expected, the ethylene concentrations increased with an increase in the pressure difference as shown in Fig. 6. As the temperature increased, the required pressure difference for enrichment of ethylene decreased. This result corresponds to the hydrogen recovery shown in Fig. 4. When the feed fl ow rate was 1000mlmin � 1 , ethylene could be enriched up to w 80%at a pressure difference of 1000 kPa and 573 K. The ability of the membrane to recover hydrogenwhile allowing for ethylene enrichment must also beconsidered. In regards to the recovered hydrogen fl ow rate, a feed rate of 1000 ml min � 1 was too low. When the feed fl ow rate and pressure difference were 2000 ml min � 1 and 1500 kPa, respectively, the ethylene concentration in the retentate reached up to 76, 80, and 82% at 573 K, 623 K, and 673 K respectively. Under these conditions, the hydrogen recovery ratio was > 96% as shown in Fig. 4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Methane was also detected in the retentate streams, which increased with temperature as shown in Fig. 7. Methane comes from the methanation reaction of carbon oxides (Eqs. (1) and (2)) [28].
texts.text: Methane was also detected in the retentate streams, which increased with temperature as shown in Fig. 7. Methane comes from the methanation reaction of carbon oxides (Eqs. (1) and (2)) [28].

However, no reduction in the hydrogen permeation fl ux was observed during the tests. SEM/EDX analysis was used to con fi rm if carbon formation occurred and the results are discussed in the following section.
texts.text: Formation of carbon, which results in methane formation, and the presence of carbon on the surface of the membrane gradually reduces the hydrogen permeation fl ux [16]. However, no reduction in the hydrogen permeation fl ux was observed during the tests. SEM/EDX analysis was used to con fi rm if carbon formation occurred and the results are discussed in the following section.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: composition was w 1wt.% as expected and there was no indication of carbon formation on the surface of the membrane as shown in Fig. 8. The membrane module consisted of a stainless steel plate and tubes. After disassembling the membrane from the membrane module, carbon black was observed in the stainless steel tubes. Stainless steel, which is composed of several kinds of metals, can act as a catalyst for the decomposition of hydrocarbons [29]. Thus, we believe that the methane formation was from ethylene decomposition in the stainless steel tubes.

Stainless steel, which is composed of several kinds of metals, can act as a catalyst for the decomposition of hydrocarbons [29]. Thus, we believe that the methane formation was from ethylene decomposition in the stainless steel tubes.

texts.text: Hydrogen permeation tests of Pd-1wt.%Au membrane with 5% ethylene mixtures, analysis of the composition of the retentate stream, and membrane analysis revealed that:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The ethylene mixture did not affect hydrogen permeation behavior when the feed streams were 3000 ml min � 1 and the pressure difference was 500 kPa at 673 K.
texts.text:  The ethylene mixture did not affect hydrogen permeation behavior when the feed streams were 3000 ml min � 1 and the pressure difference was 500 kPa at 673 K.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The effect of depletion increased with an increase in the pressure difference and temperature and an increase in the feed fl ow rate reduced the effect of depletion, which indicated that the effect of ethylene on the hydrogen permeation behavior depends only on the hydrogen partial pressure on the surface of the membranes.
texts.text:  The effect of depletion increased with an increase in the pressure difference and temperature and an increase in the feed fl ow rate reduced the effect of depletion, which indicated that the effect of ethylene on the hydrogen permeation behavior depends only on the hydrogen partial pressure on the surface of the membranes.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  At a pressure difference of 1500 kPa, a membrane with an effective area of 16.6 cm 2 could enrich 5% ethylene of 2000 ml min � 1 up to 76, 80, and 82% at 573 K, 623 K, and 673 K respectively with a hydrogen recovery ratio of > 96%.
texts.text:  At a pressure difference of 1500 kPa, a membrane with an effective area of 16.6 cm 2 could enrich 5% ethylene of 2000 ml min � 1 up to 76, 80, and 82% at 573 K, 623 K, and 673 K respectively with a hydrogen recovery ratio of > 96%.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Signi fi cant amounts of methane were detected in the retentate streams and SEM/EDX analysis revealed that the methane was from ethylene decomposition that took place not on the membrane surface but in the stainless steel tubes of the membrane module.
